ps_n01.npcDialogue: Five legions cross the pomerium at dawn. The sound comes first — thousands of hobnailed caligae striking marble, a grinding rhythm that has never been heard inside the city walls, not once in the four centuries of the Republic. Smoke is already rising from the Esquiline where Sulpicius's men tried to hold a barricade. It didn't hold. The Senate doors are chained shut from the outside, the bronze handles still warm from the hands of the last senator who tried to enter. Gaius Veturius Crispus stands in the Forum and watches the world he was born into die.

ps_n02.npcDialogue: Somewhere in a requisitioned villa, a freedman's stylus scratches across a wax tablet — names, names, names. Sulla's clerks are building the new Rome one list at a time. Men who were senators yesterday are fugitives tonight. Men who were nothing yesterday will be magistrates by the kalends. The only question that matters now is which column holds your name — the living or the dead.

ps_n03.npcDialogue: They come for you before the second watch — not soldiers but men in senatorial white, their togas freshly cleaned, as if the blood in the streets were someone else's problem. They bring you to a villa on the Palatine where oil lamps throw long shadows against maps of the city, each district marked in red or black. Sulla is at the center of it, dictating to three scribes at once, his voice flat and methodical as a man reading grain inventories. He looks up when you enter. His eyes are the color of old iron. 'Crispus. Good. I'm told you're useful and that you understand what loyalty costs.' He slides a wax tablet across the table. Your name is already inscribed — not on the proscription list, but on the other one. The one that matters.

options.playerDialogue: I'm yours. Where do you need me?
I'll serve — but there are names on your other list that don't belong there. Give me that, and you have me completely.

ps_n04.npcDialogue: A boy no older than twelve leads you through the Subura by a route that doubles back on itself three times — past a tannery that reeks of lime and urine, down a staircase so narrow your shoulders brush both walls. The cellar beneath the wine shop smells of turned earth and cheap tallow. Faces in the lamplight — Cinna, hollow-eyed, gripping a cup he hasn't drunk from. Sertorius with his hand resting on his gladius as if he's forgotten it's there. Two tribunes whose names you know and a woman you don't, copying documents onto scrolls small enough to swallow. Cinna speaks first. 'Marius is on a fishing boat bound for Africa. Sulpicius is dead — they caught him near Laurentum, a slave betrayed him for the bounty. We are what's left.' He looks at you the way a drowning man looks at driftwood.

options.playerDialogue: Then I'm what's left too. I'll hold things together until Marius returns.
I'm going after Marius. The cause needs leaders who survive, not martyrs in a cellar.
It's over, Cinna. Sulla won. The only thing left to negotiate is how many of us walk away alive.

ps_n05.npcDialogue: While Romans butcher Romans, a rider in a mud-spattered cloak finds you near the Temple of Castor. He carries a letter sealed with the stamp of the Marsic confederation — Poppaedius Silo's eagle clutching a thunderbolt. The Lex Julia promised citizenship to every Italian who laid down arms. The arms are down. The citizenship is not coming. Roman magistrates in Asculum, in Nola, in Bovianum are refusing to open the census rolls, claiming the law doesn't apply until the new consuls ratify it — consuls who answer to Sulla. The letter is brief and written in the messenger's own hand, because Poppaedius trusts no scribe with these words: 'Be our voice in whatever Rome becomes. They will listen to a Veturius. They will not listen to us.'

options.playerDialogue: Tell Poppaedius I accept. Rome made a promise. I'll make them keep it.
I'll serve the Italian cause — but from inside the magistracy, where I can force the enrollment through.

ps_n06.npcDialogue: Catulus is sitting on an overturned bench in the curia when you find him. The marble floor is gouged where legionaries dragged the bronze ballot urns out to be melted for sling-shot. A single oil lamp gutters in a chamber built for five hundred. He doesn't look up when you enter — he's staring at a crack in the wall that wasn't there yesterday, tracing it with his eyes as if reading an augury. 'We buried Scaurus this morning,' he says quietly. 'Heart gave out when the legions crossed. Probably the kindest death available.' He turns to you then, and his face has the terrible calm of a man who has decided to outlive everything he loves. 'The senate endures, Crispus. It endures because men like us sit in these chairs after the soldiers leave. Will you sit?'

options.playerDialogue: I'll sit. Someone has to remember what these chairs are for.
Only if the senate governs. I won't warm a bench in a dictator's theater — I'd rather freeze outside it.

ps_n07.npcDialogue: No knock comes at your door. No rider seeks you out. No freedman whispers your name in a corridor. The terrible truth settles like ash — no one is coming for Gaius Veturius Crispus, because no one needs him. Sulla's patrols move through the streets checking names against wax tablets, and the men who aren't on any tablet at all are the ones who vanish easiest. The Marian underground passes you without a password. The Italian delegates don't know your face. You stand in the Forum at midday and the crowd parts around you like water around a stone — not hostile, not afraid, simply indifferent. In this Rome, indifference kills.

ps_n08.npcDialogue: The new Rome assembles itself like a body after surgery — stitched, bruised, alive in ways it shouldn't be. Your name appears on the album of magistrates, inscribed in fresh wax below men who held power when power still meant something clean. The toga praetexta fits strangely, as if cut for a different man. Perhaps it was. The Republic staggers forward with a sword through its lung, and you walk beside it, one hand on its shoulder, unable to tell if you are holding it up or it is holding you.

ps_n09.npcDialogue: The silence after a failed gambit is the loudest sound in Rome. Sulla's men note your attempt and file it away — not with anger but with something worse, a mild contempt. You asked for something you hadn't earned the right to ask for, and the men who run the ledgers have long memories and sharp styluses. You aren't hunted. You aren't protected. You exist in the space between — the space where the overlooked go to be forgotten.

ps_n10.npcDialogue: You have chosen your ground. But the ground is shifting. In the third hour of night, while you are still tasting the copper of your own decision, a boy arrives at your door barefoot and out of breath. He carries no letter — the message is memorized, recited in a voice that shakes. Your name has appeared on a preliminary proscription tablet. Not confirmed, not yet carved in stone — still in soft wax, still erasable. But the wax is hardening. Dawn is four hours away. By dawn, the tablets go to the executioners.

responses.playerDialogue: Let them write it. I will not beg Sulla's clerks to unwrite what I am.
Get word to Catulus — he still has influence over the lists. Not for my sake. For the precedent it sets.
I'll go to Sulla directly. He respects a man who looks him in the face — he despises the ones who hide.
I know the freedman who keeps the tablets. A visit, a purse of silver, and my name is smudged before the wax cools.

fallbackResponse.playerDialogue: I have to leave — now, tonight, before the gates close.

ps_n11.npcDialogue: The Porta Capena at dusk. A mule loaded with everything a man can carry and still move fast. The guards barely glance at you — they are watching for bigger names, richer men, faces that appear on the tablets they can actually read. Behind you, Rome sends up a column of smoke that leans south in the evening wind, toward Campania, toward the Italian towns where a man with Roman connections and no Roman enemies might start again. You are alive. You are no one. On the Italian roads, with dust in your teeth and the stars of a republic that no longer wants you overhead — that will have to be enough.

ps_n12.npcDialogue: They find you at the third hour. Three soldiers and a clerk with a tablet, working their way through an insula near the Argiletum, checking faces against descriptions. You have no patron to invoke, no faction stamp to show, no letter of safe conduct folded in your toga. The clerk squints at you, looks down at his tablet, looks up again. 'Name?' he asks, and when you answer, he frowns — not recognition but its opposite, the irritation of a bureaucrat encountering a thing that doesn't fit his categories. He scratches something on the wax. You do not see what he writes. The soldiers take you by the arms, and the street goes on without you, as streets do.

ps_n13.npcDialogue: The days pass. Sulla's constitution settles over Rome like a new season, and seasons do not notice individual men. You walk the same streets, buy bread from the same baker, nod to the same neighbors — but the city has rearranged itself around an absence shaped like you. No summons comes. No knock. No letter sealed with anyone's ring. You attend the public assemblies and stand at the back, and when the roll is called, no one calls your name. The Republic has not destroyed Gaius Veturius Crispus. It has simply continued without him, which is the same thing, done slowly.

effects.reason: Your alliances and reputation earned you a seat in the new order

deltas.reason: Escaped Rome with your life when others did not

effects.reason: Survival through crisis has hardened you — the next phase begins with the rank your experience deserves
Without faction, patron, or reputation, you were consumed by the violence of Sulla's march on Rome
The Republic moved on without you. Neither ally nor enemy, you faded into the anonymous mass of citizens who watched Rome change and did nothing to shape it
